one_line_takeaway: A timely SoK that formalizes LLM-agent 'skills' as a four-tuple (C, pi, T, R) and uses that lens to map a seven-pattern design space and a new skill-layer threat model, arguing -- with cited evidence from the ClawHavoc marketplace attack and the SkillsBench benchmark -- that curated skills sharply boost agent success while marketplace-distributed skills open a supply-chain attack surface that binary malware scanners cannot cover.
key_finding: The skill layer is a genuinely new, poorly-defended attack surface, and the paper's tuple-level framing explains why: conventional binary scanners (e.g. VirusTotal) inspect only part of pi's executable code and none of C, T, or R's metadata semantics, so NL prompt-injection payloads and overbroad applicability conditions are labeled 'Benign'. Empirically (via the cited ClawHavoc campaign) 1,184 malicious skills reached OpenClaw's ClawHub registry within weeks, a Snyk audit found 36.8% of all published skills carried at least one security flaw, and 91% of malicious skills embedded prompt-injection payloads weaponizing the agent itself. On utility (via the cited SkillsBench benchmark), curated skills raised average pass rate by +16.2 pp (24.3%->40.6%) while self-generated skills degraded it by -1.3 pp -- concretely quantifying a curation-vs-scalability tradeoff and showing self-evolving libraries (Pattern-4) are the most scalable but least reliable acquisition mode.
core_contribution: A Systematization of Knowledge (SoK) on 'agentic skills' -- reusable, callable procedural modules for LLM agents that go beyond atomic tool calls. The paper's OWN conceptual contributions are: (1) a representation-agnostic four-tuple formalization of a skill, S = (C, pi, T, R), where C is an applicability condition, pi an executable policy, T a termination condition, and R a callable interface, explicitly paralleling the RL options framework (I, pi, beta) but adding the invocable interface R; (2) a seven-stage skill lifecycle model (discovery, practice/refinement, distillation, storage, retrieval/composition, execution, evaluation/update); (3) a seven-pattern system-level design taxonomy (metadata-driven disclosure, code-as-skill, workflow enforcement, self-evolving libraries, hybrid NL+code macros, meta-skills, marketplace distribution); (4) an orthogonal representation x scope skill-level taxonomy (five representations x six scopes); (5) a security/governance analysis contributing a six-category threat model, a four-tier trust model, a pattern-specific risk matrix, and a novel tuple-level argument for why binary malware scanners miss skill-layer attacks (each of C, pi, T, R exposes a distinct, largely unscanned attack surface). The large quantitative results it reports are synthesized from cited external sources, not the authors' own experiments.
threat_model: The paper systematizes six primary threat categories against the skill layer: (1) Poisoned skill retrieval -- adversarial metadata (SEO-poisoning analogue) makes a malicious skill rank for benign queries, exploiting Pattern-1 metadata disclosure; (2) Malicious skill payloads -- pi carries unauthorized code (supply-chain style, Pattern-2) or embedded prompt-injection instructions (Pattern-5); (3) Cross-tenant leakage -- shared skill repositories let one tenant's skill touch another tenant's data absent runtime-enforced sandboxing; (4) Skill drift exploitation -- an attacker controlling part of the environment (e.g. a web page a skill navigates) changes skill behavior without editing the skill; (5) Confused deputy via environmental injection -- untrusted observations coerce the agent into misusing an otherwise-benign privileged skill, bypassing all skill-level trust checks (a cross-cutting vector attacking the O-to-invocation data/control boundary); (6) Applicability-condition (C) poisoning -- inputs crafted so a malicious skill's C(o,g)=1 universally, activating it out of scope. Defenses proposed: a sticky four-tier trust model (T1 metadata-only, T2 instruction access, T3 supervised execution, T4 autonomous), runtime-enforced tier transitions, per-tenant sandboxing, and supply-chain governance (provenance signing, dependency auditing, continuous monitoring, version pinning).
limitations: Author-acknowledged: (1) corpus recency -- most analyzed systems are from 2023-2024, so patterns may not be stable and taxonomies may need revision; (2) corpus coverage -- only 24 systems deeply analyzed from 65 retained; industry, non-English, and concurrent-preprint work may be missed; (3) taxonomy validation -- the seven patterns are bottom-up-derived and not validated by external expert survey or formal concept analysis, and their deliberate non-exclusivity complicates classification. Reviewer-noted: the paper's headline quantitative claims are NOT its own experiments -- they are drawn almost entirely from a single non-peer-reviewed benchmark (SkillsBench), which the authors themselves flag as needing independent replication, and from third-party security reports on ClawHavoc/OpenClaw whose figures cannot be independently verified from the paper. Domain-variance interpretations are presented as hypotheses with possible confounders (task construction, verifier strictness, authoring quality). No new defensive mechanism is implemented or evaluated by the authors; mitigations are recommendations.

datasets_used: SkillsBench (cited benchmark, not authors' own): 86 tasks across 11 domains (healthcare, manufacturing, cybersecurity, natural science, energy, finance, office work, media, robotics, mathematics, software engineering), 7 agent-model configurations, 7,308 agent trajectories, each task run under no-skills / curated-skills / self-generated-skills with deterministic verifiers
ClawHavoc / OpenClaw ClawHub registry incident data (cited third-party security reports from Snyk and VirusTotal)
Survey corpus: ~180 candidate papers screened to 65 retained, 24 systems mapped in depth (paper's own corpus, mapped in Tables 2 and 5)
Agent Skills Guard / SkillGuard controlled evaluation (cited): 39 test cases including 4 adversarial samples that VirusTotal marked Benign
quantitative_results: SkillsBench (cited): curated skills raise average pass rate +16.2 pp, from 24.3% to 40.6%
SkillsBench (cited): self-generated skills change pass rate -1.3 pp vs no-skills baseline; only 1 of 5 configs improved (Claude Opus 4.6 +1.4 pp); Codex + GPT-5.2 degraded -5.6 pp
SkillsBench (cited) domain variance: healthcare +51.9 pp, manufacturing +41.9 pp, cybersecurity +23.2 pp, software engineering +4.5 pp, mathematics +6.0 pp
SkillsBench (cited): focused skills of 2-3 modules +18.6 pp vs 4+ skills +5.9 pp; 'detailed' skills +18.8 pp vs 'comprehensive' documentation -2.9 pp
SkillsBench (cited): Claude Haiku 4.5 with skills 27.7% outperforms Claude Opus 4.5 without skills 22.0% (skills as compute equalizer)
SkillsBench (cited): 16 of 84 tasks degrade with skills, worst case -39.3 pp
ClawHavoc (cited): 1,184 malicious skills identified in ClawHub; Snyk audit found 36.8% of all published skills had >=1 security flaw

ClawHavoc (cited): 12 publisher accounts, one account responsible for 677 packages (57% of malicious listings); top skill 'What Would Elon Do' had 9 vulnerabilities (2 critical) with ranking inflated by 4,000 faked downloads

ClawHavoc (cited): Snyk found 283 of 3,984 skills (7.1%) exposed plaintext credentials; VirusTotal analyzed 3,016+ skills; 91% of malicious skills embedded prompt-injection payloads; 135,000+ exposed OpenClaw instances across 82 countries; AMOS payload targeted 60+ crypto wallet types
ClawHub 36.8% malicious-skill rate stated to be orders of magnitude worse than mature registries such as npm/pip
Agent Skills Guard/SkillGuard (cited): reputation score 0-100 with >80 'safe' / <30 'malicious' bands; no false positives on legitimate skills in the 39-case test set
Paper's own corpus statistics: ~180 candidates -> 65 retained -> 24 deeply analyzed; systems use a median of 2 design patterns (range 1-4); most common combination Patterns 1+7 in 4 systems
baselines_compared: Not applicable in the experimental sense -- this is a SoK with no method of its own to benchmark. Comparative axes used conceptually: skills vs tools vs plans vs episodic memory vs prompt templates (Table 1); skill formalization vs the RL options framework (I, pi, beta) and classical HTN/BDI/STRIPS-PDDL planning; the seven design patterns compared across context cost, determinism, composability, governance (Table 4). Within the cited SkillsBench evidence, the comparison baselines are no-skills vs curated-skills vs self-generated-skills conditions.
